title: A Pfaffian quantum Hall state of ultracold bosons
authors: Joyce Kwan, Perrin Segura, Yanfei Li, Tizian Blatz, Annie Zhi, Brice Bakkali-Hassani, Annabelle Bohrdt, Martin Greiter, Fabian Grusdt, Markus Greiner
abstract: Fractional quantum Hall states are a cornerstone of topological physics, hosting fractionally charged quasiparticles with exotic statistics that promise to enable topologically protected quantum information processing. Among these, the Pfaffian state introduced by Moore and Read implements a p-wave pairing structure that supports excitations with non-Abelian exchange statistics. Despite extensive study in electronic systems, direct access to its pairing structure has remained limited. Here we realize a three-particle bosonic Pfaffian state of ultracold $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$ atoms in an optical lattice subject to a Floquet-engineered synthetic magnetic field. Using a Bayesian-optimized adiabatic protocol, we prepare a state exhibiting Pfaffian pairing correlations. Site-resolved measurements of multi-point density correlations reveal a pronounced suppression of short-range three-body coincidences, reflecting the underlying pairing structure. We further probe the state's transport response through Hall drift measurements. Our results establish a bottom-up approach to engineering non-Abelian topological order and lay the groundwork for future explorations of anyonic braiding in synthetic matter.
summary: The research demonstrates the bottom-up engineering of a Pfaffian quantum Hall state using ultracold bosonic atoms in an optical lattice. By employing optimized adiabatic ramps and measuring correlation functions and transport properties, the authors provide strong evidence for non-Abelian topological order. This work establishes cold atoms as a powerful platform to study exotic many-body physics.
main_problem: The primary goal is to experimentally realize and characterize a Pfaffian quantum Hall state using ultracold bosons, which hosts non-Abelian topological order.
main_result: The authors successfully prepared the three-particle bosonic Pfaffian state by optimizing adiabatic ramps and provided evidence through suppressed short-range three-body density correlations and measurable Hall drift plateaus.
method: State preparation involves a multi-stage, Bayesian-optimized adiabatic ramp sequence in an optical lattice. Characterization relies on site-resolved measurements of two- and three-point density correlation functions and transport measurements (Hall drift).
model_or_system: The system consists of ultracold $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$ bosons confined in an optical lattice subjected to a Floquet-engineered synthetic magnetic field. The physics is modeled using the interacting Harper–Hofstadter Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Two- and three-point density correlations ($g(2), g(3)$), connected three-body correlations ($g^{(3,c)}$), Hall drift velocity ($v_\perp$), and energy gap ($\Delta E$).
important_parameters: Low temperatures ($T \lesssim 0.24 	ext{ J}$), specific flux values ($\phi/2\pi = 0.21$ for Pfaffian), and optimized ramp parameters.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the assumption that post-selection based on three atoms on distinct sites is representative of the full many-body state, and disorder reconstruction methods involve averaging potentially non-unique potential reconstructions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the analytic wave function overlap (Fig. 1), the preparation sequence and gap mapping (Fig. 2), density distributions showing suppression of $g(3)$ in the Pfaffian state (Fig. 3), and Hall drift measurements confirming plateaus for different topological regimes (Fig. 5, Fig. 7).
paper_structure: The paper details the theoretical model (Hamiltonian/Pfaffian wave function), describes the experimental setup using optical lattices and synthetic fields, outlines the state preparation protocol via Bayesian optimization of adiabatic ramps, and finally presents diagnostics using correlation functions and transport measurements to confirm topological order.
why_interesting: This work represents a cutting-edge demonstration of engineering complex topological phases in controllable quantum simulators. The combination of advanced control techniques (Floquet engineering, Bayesian optimization) with rigorous diagnostic tools (correlation functions, transport) makes it highly relevant for AMO physicists studying interacting many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Three-particle Pfaffian state of ultracold bosons in an optical lattice. a, The analytic wave function proposed in equation (2) describes a three-particle bosonic Pfaffian state. While the full Pfaffian wave function describes a p-wave paired many-body state, in the three-particle case one particle remains unpaired and the wave function is mod- ified by a finite-size correction. The resulting wave function describes vortex motion between the pair and the unpaired single particle. b, By engineering a synthetic magnetic field, we realize this state with three charge-neutral 87Rb atoms in an optical lattice, confined to 5 × 5 sites. The system is de- scribed by the interacting bosonic...

FIG. 2. Preparation of a minimal bosonic Pfaffian state. a, Experimental sequence. Shown are schematics of the state in the Harper–Hofstadter Hamiltonian (top), the corresponding ground-state density from exact diagonalization (middle), and the measured in-situ density (bottom). The sequence consists of three stages: initialization of three localized atoms on a 5 × 5 lattice, delocalization along y into a 1D tube, and delocalization along x into the final 2D Pfaffian state. Panels (3a) and (3b) show densities near the end of the final ramp stage. b, Energy gap between the ground and first excited states as a function of the tunneling amplitude along x, K, and the residual tilt along x, ∆x,...

FIG. 3. Particle densities and correlations in the Pfaffian and normal regimes. a, Measured density distributions on the 5 × 5 lattice (top) compared with ground-state theory (bottom). b, Radial average of the density distribution. For the three-particle Pfaffian state, defined in equation (2), depletion at the central site is clearly visible, consistent with the chiral structure induced by the synthetic magnetic field. Solid lines show ground-state theory. Error bars denote s.e.m. c, Two- and three-point density correlation functions probing the pairing structure of the Pfaffian state. The data show clear suppression of g(3) at short distances, consistent with Pfaffian pairing, and agree...

FIG. 4. Analysis of binned particle configurations. Site-resolved imaging probes the paired structure of the Pfaf- fian wave function using the exact particle positions. a, Im- ages containing a nearest-neighbor pair are grouped into bins according to the bond-center distance d, defined as the dis- tance between the third particle and the pair’s center of mass. Each bin is represented by a line connecting three lattice sites on the 5 × 5 grid. Configurations related by mirror or rota- tional symmetry are assigned to the same bin. Colors indicate the number of particles in the bulk: three (dark purple), two (mid purple), or one or zero (light purple). Bins are labeled by the (x, y)...

FIG. 5. The Hall drift experiment. Transport response of the Pfaffian state probed through center-of-mass drift. a, Experimental protocol: (1) prepare the Pfaffian state within the repulsive walls; (2) lower the confinement and apply a force along the x direction; (3) measure the density profile after an evolution time t. b, Measured density profiles with an applied tilt (top) compared with theory (bottom). In the presence of a force, the density drifts in the transverse y direction, whereas it remains stationary over the same evolution time when no force is applied (right). c, Transverse center-of-mass drift.
